October 19, 1992
James C. Jeffery III
ENGINEER]NG CONSULTING SERVICES
P.O. Box 1492
Santa Cruz, California

RE: Conflict of Interest - City Contractors
Dear Mr. Jeffery:
This letter will respond to your October 9, 1992 letter. 
In
that Ietter, you referenced an earlier tel,ephone conversation j-n
which, according to the Ietter, I advised you that because you sat
on the City's Board of Auilding Appeals, confl,ict of interest
regulations would preclude you fron performing contract work for
the City of Santa Cruz. 
This is not my recollection of the
conversation. 
I recall you asking whether or not you could
represent business clients before City boards and commissions lrhile
holding a seat on the Board of Buil.ding Appeals. The answer to the
latter question is, in rnost circumstances, no. The answer to the
former question is that a person who performs contract work
directly for the City, by virtue of that fact alone, does not have
a conflict of interest i-f he or she sits on a City board or
commission. In fact, a number of City empl,oyees have sat on
various City boards and commissions in the past.
For your future reference, I will summarize the applicable larv
for professional- consultants such as yourself.
Government Code S87100 provides that no public official at any
level of 1oca1 government shal1 make or in any way attempt to use
his official position to influence governmental decisions in which
he has a financial interest. 
pursuant to Governnent Code S87103,
the official 
has a financj,al interest j-n a governmental decision
when it is reasonably foreseeable that the government decisi_on wil-l-
have a material financial- impact upon hlm or a member of his
immediate family. 
I\mong other things, a decision is considered to
have a material financial effect when that decision has the
potential to result in income of 5250.00 or more to the public
of f i-ciar. 2 california Admini-strative code s18702.1. an ofiiciar
is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, 
for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official 
appears before any officer of that a-gency. ,,Attempts to
influence" include appearances by the official -on behalf of a

James C. Jeffery lII

client. 
2 California Administrative Code 518700.1.
An official 
is not attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if the official
appears in the same manner as any other member of the general
public before an agency soJ,eJ.y to represent himself on a matter
which j-s related to his personal interests. 
SecondJ.y, an official
is not attempting to use his or her official position to influence
a governmental decision if the official 
prepares drawings or
submissions of an architecturaL, engineering or sirnilar nature to
be used by a client in connection with a proceeding before the
agency, However, this exception applies only if the official 
has
nc other direct oral or wri-uten contact wiih the agency regarding
the client's project except for necessary contact with agency staff
concerning the processing or evaluation of the drawings or
submissions prepared by the official. 
ThirdJ.y, an official 
is not
attempting to use his or her official 
position to influence a
governmental position if the official- appears before a design or
architectural review corurittee or similar body of which he or she
is a member to present drawi-ngs or submissions of an architectural,
engineering or similar nature which the official- has prepared for
a client if: (1) the review corunittee's sole function is to revj.ew
architectural or engineering plans or designs and to 
make
recommendatj-ons concerning those plans or designs to a Planning
Coru[ission or other agency; and (2) the ordinance or other
provision of law pursuant to which the review committee is
established requires the review committee to include architects,
engineers or person6 in related professions, and the official 
was
appointed to the body to fulfill 
this requirement; and (3) the
official 
is a sole practitioner.
with regard to the first 
referenced exception, you could
represent yourself before the Board of Building Appeals in a matter
related to property you own. With regard to the second exception,
you wouJ,d be authorized to prepare drawings or subnissions of an
engineering naLure to be used by your client in connection with a
proceeding before the Board of Building Appeals. However, you
couLd have no other direct oral or written contact with the Board
pertaining to the client's project. 
Fina11y, the third exception
does not apply. If you are a sole practitioner, this exception
would only apply if the Board of Building Appeal's sole function
was to review architectural or engineering plans or designs and to
rnake recommendations with regard thereto and, secondly, only if the
ordinance or oLher provision of law establishing the Board of
Building Appeals required an engineer to sit thereon and you were
appointed pursuant to that provision. A review of the oidinance
code and the Zoning Board bylaws reveal that while the 1atter
requirement can be met (Chapter 18.41 of the MunicipaL Code
requires engineering professionals to sit on the Board), the former
cannot. In light of the foregoing, as a member of the Board of
Building Appeals, you could not appear before that body on behalf


ATCITISON, A-\DERSON, HTIRLEY & ITAIIISONE
we have been advised
Poli-tical Practices Corunittee
James C. Jeffery III
Page 3
of your clients.
The next issue is whether this prohibition would likewise
forbid you from appearing before the City Council, Planning
Commission or Zoning Board on behalf of your clients in an effort
to obtain approval of projects for which you provided engineering
services.
bya
that
staff attorney for the Fair
inasmuch as Board and Con[lission
members such as yoursel-f were appointed to these positions by the
City Council, thelz are pr:ecluded from appearing before the City
Council, Planning Commission or Zoning Board in an effort to
influence decisions on projects in which they have a material
financial interest. 
The issue here is whether, when appearing
before the City Council, Planning Commission or Zoning Board, you
would be appeari-ng before your "agency". The Fair Political
Practices Commission takes the position that the Iocal government
agency in this case is the City of Santa Cruz. As a member of the
City's Board of Building Appeals, you are considered an official of
the City of Santa Cruz. On the basis of thi-s legal constructi-on,
the Fair Political Practices Commission advises that, in addj-tion
to being precluded from appearing before the Board of Building
Appeals in an attmept to inf l-uence governmental decisions in which
you have a material financial interest, you are similarly precluded
from appearing before the City CounciI, Zoning Board or Plannj-ng
Commission, In researching this area of law, I have come across no
case 1aw or legi-slative history which would lead me to believe that
the Fair Political Practices Commission has erroneously cone to
this conclusion. Accordingly, I must agree with the Fair pol-iticaf
Practices Comrnission's interpretation.
In sunmary, our analysis of the applicable provisions of the
California Government Code and California Administrative Code Leads
me to conclude +-hat you may ncr- appear bef ore +-he Zonr-ng Board,
Planning Commission or City Council on behalf of your clients with
regard to your clients' projects

sections.question_synthetic: A lawyer is responding to a client's inquiry about conflict of interest rules governing a city board member who provides engineering consulting services to clients with business before the city.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This appears to be incoming correspondence (a lawyer's advisory letter to a client) rather than an FPPC response letter. The document references advice from FPPC staff but is not written by FPPC. No formal question/conclusion sections. The document contains substantive legal analysis and exceptions related to Government Code §87100 and §87103. Confidence is moderate due to unclear document provenance - it's a law firm letter discussing FPPC positions but not an official FPPC advice letter.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: A lawyer is responding to a client's inquiry about conflict of interest rules governing a city board member who provides engineering consulting services to clients with business before the city.

embedding.summary: This is a lawyer's letter (Atchison, Anderson, Hurley & Barisone) responding to James C. Jeffery III, a member of Santa Cruz's Board of Building Appeals and engineering consultant, clarifying conflict of interest rules under California Government Code. The lawyer advises that Jeffery cannot appear before the Board of Building Appeals, City Council, Planning Commission, or Zoning Board on behalf of clients regarding projects where he has a material financial interest, except in limited circumstances.
